comments.text: Here is a small observation on the corrected “multiples” version of #488. It may already be known, but I did not see it stated explicitly in the thread. Let\[ F_A(x)=|\{1\le t\le x:\ a\mid t\text{ for some }a\in A\}|. \]I claim that the conjectured inequality holds when \(A\) has two elements. Write \(A=\{a,b\}\), where \(2\le a<b\). First suppose \(a\nmid b\). Then \(b\) is counted and is not a multiple of \(a\), so for every \(n\ge b\),\[ F_A(n)\ge \lfloor n/a\rfloor+1\ge (n+1)/a. \]On the other hand \(F_A(m)\le m/a+m/b<2m/a\), and hence\[ \frac{F_A(m)}{m}<\frac{2}{a}\le \frac{2F_A(n)}{n+1}<\frac{2F_A(n)}{n}. \]If \(a\mid b\), then the counted set is just the multiples of \(a\), so \(F_A(x)=\lfloor x/a\rfloor\). Since \(n\ge b\ge 2a\), we have \(\lfloor n/a\rfloor\ge2\). Thus\[ 2mF_A(n)-nF_A(m) \ge m\left(2\lfloor n/a\rfloor-\frac{n}{a}\right)>0, \]which gives the desired strict inequality. So #488 holds for every two-element set \(A\). The near-sharp examples are already visible here: for \(A=\{a,a+1\}\), \(n=2a-1\), and \(m=a^2\),\[ \frac{F_A(m)/m}{F_A(n)/n} = \frac{(2a-1)^2}{2a^2}, \]which tends to \(2\) from below. This leaves the first genuinely nontrivial case at primitive sets of size at least \(3\), where the overlaps between multiple sets start to matter. AI-usage disclosure: I found this while working with Codex/ChatGPT-style tools

For the split-core tripod family $A=\{2u,3v,uv\}$ with $\gcd(u,v)=1$, $v\geq 3$ odd, $u\geq 4$, and $3\nmid u$, EP-488 holds: $B_A(n,m) := 2m\,F_A(n) - n\,F_A(m) > 0$ for all integers $m > n \geq uv$. This includes every prime witness tripod $\{2p,3q,pq\}$ with distinct odd primes $p,q>3$. The family is primitive but not $2$-primitive, since $uv$ divides $(2u)(3v)$; a first-pass literature search located no explicit prior treatment of this exact family. Note (PDF, 5 pages): note Prepared with 5.5 Pro; the math has been verified by hand and by an independent audit script.

One notable thing about this problem - unusual for an Erdos problem - is that there are several ways to make the problem easier to prove, yet the problem remains difficult. For instance, here are four ways to reduce the difficulty level: Take the asymptotic limit $m \to \infty$. Take the asymptotic limit $\max(A) = o(n)$ (and perhaps replace $2$ by $(2+o(1))$ to be able to take advantage of this). Replace the factor $2$ by an unspecified constant $C$. Assume that $A$ consists entirely of primes. What I find remarkable is that even when enabling multiple of these "cheats", the problem is still nontrivial. I can resolve the problem with Cheats 1, 3, and 4 enabled: that is to say I can show that $$ \lim_{m \to \infty} \frac{|B \cap [1,m]|}{m} \ll \frac{|B \cap [1,n]}{n}$$ when $A$ consists entirely of primes. Here the left-hand side is $1 - \prod_{p \in A} (1-\frac{1}{p})$. When $\sum_p \frac{1}{p}$ is small the right-hand side is $\asymp \sum_p \frac{1}{p}$ by the Bonferroni inequalities, and then when $\sum_p \frac{1}{p}$ is large the RHS is $\asymp 1$ by monotonicity, so in either case thed claim follows. But I don't see a way to prove this version in a way that suggests how to remove more of the cheats.

Progress on EP-488: \(a=2\) case proved, Chojecki reduction fails for \(a\geq3\) Some results on the density-doubling conjecture, building on the pair-tail decomposition framework from Conjecture 4.8. 1. The \(a=2\) case. For every primitive set \(A\) with \(2 \in A\) (equivalently, \(\min(A)=2\)), the conjecture holds. Since every element of \(A\) is \(\geq 2\), integer 1 is never counted, so \(F(m) \leq m-1\) and \(F(m)/m < 1\) for all \(m\). If \(A \neq \{2\}\), choose \(b \in A \setminus \{2\}\). Since \(A\) is primitive and \(2 \in A\), this \(b\) is necessarily odd. For \(n \geq b\):\[ F(n) \geq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + \left\lfloor \frac{n}{b} \right\rfloor - \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2b} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + \#\{\text{odd multiples of } b \leq n\} > \frac{n}{2}. \]Therefore \(2F(n)/n > 1 > F(m)/m\). For the singleton \(A = \{2\}\): \(F(k) = \lfloor k/2 \rfloor\), so for all \(m > n \geq 2\),\[ \frac{2F(n)}{n} = \frac{2\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}{n} > \frac{1}{2} \geq \frac{\lfloor m/2 \rfloor}{m} = \frac{F(m)}{m}. \]This covers all primitive sets with \(2 \in A\). **2. The Chojecki reduction fails for \(a \geq 3\).** The reduction to a fixed threshold \(s\) (first integer with \(F(s) \geq 5\)) does not extend to \(a \geq 3\). Counterexample family: For any \(a \geq 3\), take \(b = 5a+1\) and \(T = \{t \in \mathbb{Z} : 5a+1 < t \leq 10a+1,\; a \nmid t\}\). Then \(A = \{a,b\} \cup T\) is primitive: every element of \(\{b\} \cup T\) lies in \((5a,\, 10a+1]\), so each exceeds \((10a+1)/2\) and no two can divide each other; and \(a\) divides none of them by construction. The first \(s\) with \(F(s) \geq 5\) is \(s = 5a\), since below \(5a\), only multiples of \(a\) contribute and there are exactly 4 of them (\(a, 2a, 3a, 4a\)). So \(F(s) = 5\) and \(2F(s)/s = 2/a\). At \(m = 10a+1\): every integer in \([5a+1,\, 10a+1]\) is counted by \(A\) (divisible by \(a\) \(\to\) counted; not divisible by \(a\) \(\to\) in \(T\) or equals \(b\) \(\to\) counted). So \(F(10a+1) = 5 + (5a+1) = 5a+6\), giving\[ \frac{F(m)}{m} = \frac{5a+6}{10a+1} > \frac{2}{a} = \frac{2F(s)}{s} \]since \(5a^2 - 14a - 2 > 0\) for all \(a \geq 3\). Concrete: \(a=3\), \(A = \{3,16,17,19,20,22,23,25,26,28,29,31\}\), \(F(31)/31 \approx 0.677 > 2/3\). Note: this does NOT disprove EP-488, since \(s = 5a < \max(A) = 10a+1\), so the counterexample lies outside the valid range \(n \geq \max(A)\). 3. What remains. The original EP-488 statement (all \(m > n \geq \max(A)\)) is checked computationally across 25,000+ primitive systems with zero failures and margins \(\geq 33\%\) (defined as \((2F(n)/n - F(m)/m) / (2F(n)/n)\) for the tightest \((n,m)\) pair). The reduction to sieve oscillation still works within the \(n \geq \max(A)\) regime, where the key inequality becomes: for any finite \(Q \subset \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 2}\) and \(x \geq \max(Q)\), is \(A_Q(x) < 2\delta_Q x\)? For pairwise coprime \(Q\), Hall–Hildebrand type bounds on the complementary unsieved count operate at an \(e^\gamma\)-scale; translating that into the form \(A_Q(x) < 2\delta_Q x\) requires care. For non-coprime \(Q\), computational evidence shows strictly smaller oscillation — the lcm lattice produces 30–70% coefficient cancellation in the inclusion-exclusion.

The commentary gives an example where $A$ is a singleton of size around $n/2$, and the ratio is close to 2. There is a somewhat different example where $A$ now consists of numbers much smaller than $n$, and the ratio is about 1.03; nowhere near a counterexample, but still uniformly bounded away from 1, and indicative of the complexity of the problem. The counterexample comes from taking $A$ to be the primes between $n^{1/3}$ and $n^{1/2}$, and $m$ to be extremely large compared to $n$. The density at $m$ is then about $1 - \prod_{n^{1/3} < p < n^{1/2}} (1-\frac{1}{p})$, which by Mertens' theorem is about $1/3 = 0.333\dots$. The density at $n$ can be computed by inclusion-exclusion to be about $\sum_{n^{1/3} < p < n^{1/2}} 1/p - \sum_{n^{1/3} < p_1 < p_2 < n^{1/2}} 1/p_1 p_2$ which computes to $\log(3/2) - \frac{1}{2} \log^2(3/2) = 0.32326413$. The ratio here is $1.03114853\dots$. Some tweaking of this type of example seems to permit a slightly larger ratio than this, but it doesn't look like it gets close to 2. Proving this concretely looks tricky though. In the spirit of this paper of Granville-Soundararajan, the main task involves proving an inequality involving alternating sums of various integrals, which looks somewhat complicated.

Your example with primes between \(n^{1/3}\) and \(n^{1/2}\) lines up well with what I see in a systematic computational search. Across 6,207 primitive pairs \((a,b,T)\) with \(F(s) \geq 5\), the peak density ratio \(\sup_{m>s} \frac{F(m)/m}{\delta}\) falls in the range \([1.035, 1.373]\), with the highest values coming from systems where the tail \(T\) contains many small primes consistent with your construction. Over 1.15 million per-residue checks found zero violations of the split inequality. On the structural side, I found an exact identity that may be relevant. Writing \(Q_\ell, Q_a, Q_b\) for the quotient-tail families, the Möbius subtraction \(F(n) = A_{Q_a}(\lfloor n/a\rfloor) + A_{Q_b}(\lfloor n/b\rfloor) - A_{Q_\ell}(\lfloor n/\ell\rfloor)\) rewrites as a sum of three non-negative counting functions:\[F(n) = A_{Q_\ell}(w) + A_{\min(Q_a \cup \{A\})}(u) + A_{\min(Q_b \cup \{B\})}(v)\]where \(A = \ell/a\), \(B = \ell/b\), and \(w, u, v\) are the respective quotients. (Verified across 1,805 systems with zero failures.) This eliminates the cancellation that makes the original three-term formula hard to bound. Combined with a bridge lemma splitting active vs. inactive moduli, this reduces the full inequality to bounding the positive oscillation \(W^+_{Q_{\leq y}}/y\) of the active sieve at the ratio peak. Computationally this holds across 2,648 systems with 69%+ margins, but proving it is exactly where things get stuck, 13 natural approaches have been killed with explicit counterexamples. The full reduction chain with verification code is written up; happy to share if useful. Would be very curious whether the alternating integral framework you mention could handle the non-multiplicative case that arises here.

I had a pretty long back-and-forth with GPT-5.4 about the problem, generally proving a bunch of special cases. I attach the full note with results here, but also check what the new Aristotle have done here when it comes to formalization (multiple files + readme). It's pretty good on this problem which is elementary, but has tricky computations and notations that LLMs are worse at. I've done GPT-5.4 to Aristotle and back loop a couple of times too. Also I couldn't find much literature when it comes to this problem.
Thanks! Standard check claims 1 minor issue.
Thank you, I've actually did many many rewrites of this note with GPT-5.4 and never could get it perfect. But Aristotle on the other hand and autoformalization works amazingly well here.

Is it too long? You may want to focus on a single part (e.g. section) at a time. Another tip is to do the diff between new and old versions (if the whole paper is long and the diff is relatively short it helps to focus).
Yes, probably because of the length, anything over 10 pages is bound to have some small issues (still). Fortunately results were also verified by Aristotle and are in Lean so I'm confident here.

Theoretically any length should be doable by working hierarchically (up to what's reasonable but I think e.g. 50 pages are doable with GPT-5.4). But you need to add structure - probably similar to structures you'd use yourself when you write it but which the AI hasn't learned (due to the fact that tasks up to a few hours are already frontier-ish as a target, and these are beyond those tasks).

Progress on Conjecture 4.8 (pair-tail split doubling) and a specific remaining gap Hi Mr. Chojecki, I've been working on the pair-tail split doubling conjecture (Conjecture 4.8 in your paper) using the quotient-tail framework from your Section 8, and I've made significant progress but hit a specific wall. I wanted to share what I have and ask whether you see a path through the remaining gap. What I've proved Starting from the quotient-tail decomposition $$F_{a,b\mid T}(x) = A_{Q_a}\!\left(\left\lfloor\frac{x}{a}\right\rfloor\right) + A_{Q_b}\!\left(\left\lfloor\frac{x}{b}\right\rfloor\right) - A_{Q_\ell}\!\left(\left\lfloor\frac{x}{\ell}\right\rfloor\right),$$ I built a reduction chain: 1. Exact periodicity. $F(n) = \delta n + c_{n \bmod P}$ with $\delta_{a,b\mid T} = \frac{\delta(Q_a)}{a} + \frac{\delta(Q_b)}{b} - \frac{\delta(Q_\ell)}{\ell} > 0$. 2. Visible-slab reduction. The finite-window split inequality reduces to checks (V1), (V2), (V3) on the base visible slab $S_{\mathrm{vis}} = [N_0, \min(N_1, P-1)] \cap \mathbb{Z}$. 3. Low-$F$ elimination. All visible-slab residues with $F(s) = 2$ or $F(s) = 3$ are automatically harmless (the threshold $2F(s)/s > 1/a + 1/b$ suffices). $F(s) = 4$ reduces to an explicit exceptional family, which I closed completely — including the sub-exceptional scaling family $(48k, 54k, \{64k, 72k, 81k\})$ via a scaling law: the quotient tails $\hat{Q}_{48k} = \hat{Q}_{54k} = \{3,4\}$ are $k$-independent, so $F_k(qk+t) = F_1(q)$, collapsing the infinite family to a finite base computation on 5103 residues. 4. Window-bound theorem. For any window length $L$, $F(n) - F(n-L) \leq c(L)$ with $c(L)/L \to \delta$. This gives an inductive propagation principle that eliminates envelope comparisons beyond the visible slab. So the split inequality holds for all pair-tail systems where every visible-slab residue has $F(s) \leq 4$, plus the specific exceptional families at $F = 4$. The remaining gap: $F(s) \geq 5$ on the visible slab The endgame reduces to: for every visible-slab $s$ with $F(s) \geq 5$ and every $m > s$, prove $$\frac{F(m)}{m} < \frac{2F(s)}{s}.$$ I've verified this computationally across tens of thousands of systems with zero counterexamples. The margins are consistently 30–50% of the slab ratio. What I've tried and what fails: - Universal bound $F(m)/m \leq \delta + W^+/m$ combined with $2F(s)/s \geq 10/s$: closes 98.6% of systems but fails when $3W^+ + |W^-| \geq 10$. - Per-residue bound $W^+ < 2F(s) - \delta s$: false. Counterexample at $(11, 15 \mid \{28, 31\})$, $s = 43$. - Combinatorial charging ($\Delta_a + \Delta_b \geq 2\Delta_\ell + 1$): the strong form is false. - Two-route via Proposition 5.1: killed by $G = $ first 14 primes at $n = 198$. The split inequality holds in every case, but every bound overshoots because the global $W^+$ is achieved at a different residue than the ratio peak $\sup_m F(m)/m$. The structural picture The inequality appears to be "soft" — it holds because the factor of 2 creates a multiplicative buffer that the periodic corrections cannot fully consume. At the ratio-maximizing $m^*$, the correction $c_{m^*}$ is always much less than $W^+$ (typically $c_{m^*}/W^+ \approx 0.3$–$0.5$). But I cannot prove this universally. Questions 1. Do you see a way to prove $F(m)/m < 2F(s)/s$ for $F(s) \geq 5$ using the quotient-tail structure? Perhaps through the cluster expansion / Janson bound machinery from your Section 8? 2. Is there a known technique for bounding $c_m/m$ (the ratio peak of periodic corrections) rather than $W^+/m$? 3. Do you think Conjecture 4.8 is provable by this route, or does it require a fundamentally different approach for large tails? Happy to share the full proof chain, computational code, and counterexamples to the failed bounds.
It would great if you could share the full pdf/tex file + computations!
Here they are! Thanks for Engaging with this your help is much appreciated. PDF Computation
Thanks! I guess this computational search paired with Terence Tao remark below, should result in something new, but I don't know if this would give full resolution (but maybe).
I think this statement is clearly false as written. In the definition of $B$, shouldn't we require "$a \mid n$ for some $a \in A$" rather than "$a \mid n$ for all $a \in A$"?
Yes, this was just a typo (the set of multiples of $A$ was intended).

[Note: this comment was written before 2025/12/01, when the problem text was updated.] I agree that the problem in [Er61,p.236] is correctly transcribed here. However, consider the full context: "Let $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots \le n$ be any sequence of integers, $b_1 < b_2 < \cdots$ the integers no one of which is a multiple of any $a$. $B(x) = \sum_{b_i\le x} 1$. Is it true that for every $m > n$ $\frac{B(m)}{m} < 2 \frac{B(n)}{n}?$ It is easy to see that in [that equation] $2$ can not be replaced by any smaller constant, to see this let the $a$'s consist of $a_1$, $n = 2 a_1 - 1$, $m = 2 a_1$." The example at the end doesn't make sense for this problem. However, it makes perfect sense for the problem where the $b_i$s actually consist of the *multiples* of elements in A, not the non-multiples. Morever, that version has no small counterexamples. Indeed, for small $n$ and $m$, the limiting extremal examples are precisely the ones Erdős describes with $n=2a_1-1 < 2a_1 = m$. In summary, I think a new problem should be added (if it's not present already! -- I couldn't find it) where non-multiples is changed to multiples! (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Assuming the following inequality holds, this version of the problem is straightforward. For $A=\{a_1<\ldots<a_k\}\subset \mathbb{N}$ and an integer $n\geq a_k$, assume we have $$\sum_{\emptyset
eq I=\{a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r}\}\subseteq A} (-1)^{r+1} ig\lfloor rac{n}{lcm(a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r})} igfloor\geq -1+ \sum_{\emptyset
eq I=\{a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r}\}\subseteq A}(-1)^{r+1}  rac{n}{lcm(a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r})}.$$ Then by the IEP, $B(n):=|B\cap[1,n]|=\sum_{\emptyset
eq I=\{a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r}\}\subseteq A} (-1)^{r+1} ig\lfloor rac{n}{lcm(a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r})} igfloor$ so for $m>n\geq a_k$, $$ rac{B(n)}{n}= \sum_{\emptyset
eq I=\{a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r}\}\subseteq A}  rac{(-1)^{r+1}}{n} ig\lfloor rac{n}{lcm(a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r})} igfloor \geq  rac{-1}{n}+ \sum_{\emptyset
eq I=\{a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r}\}\subseteq A}(-1)^{r+1}  rac{1}{lcm(a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r})}$$ $$\geq  rac{-1}{n}+\sum_{\emptyset
eq I= rac{-1}{n}+\{a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r}\}\subseteq A}  rac{(-1)^{r+1}}{m} ig\lfloor rac{m}{lcm(a_{i_1},\ldots,a_{i_r})} igfloor= rac{-1}{n}+ rac{B(m)}{m}.$$ So $ rac{B(n)+1}{n}\geq  rac{B(m)}{m}$, and if $B(n)>1$, then $ rac{2B(n)}{n}> rac{B(n)+1}{n}\geq  rac{B(m)}{m}$. When $B(n)=1$, we have $A=\{a\}$ and $a\leq n<2a$. It is clear that $ rac{2B(n)}{n}= rac{2}{n}> rac{1}{a}$ and that for any $k\in\mathbb{N}$ and $ka\leq m<(k+1)a$, we have $ rac{B(m)}{m}= rac{k}{m}\leq  rac{1}{a}$. So $ rac{2B(n)}{n}> rac{B(m)}{m}$.

Regarding "the following inequality", the difference is actually rather big. Take $A=\{x+1,x+2, \ldots, x+k\}$ and $n=2x$. Then for $x$ sufficiently large. We have $LHS=k$ and $RHS=-1+2k-o(1)$. Also the cardinalities of $B \cap [1,n]$ and $B \cap [k+1,k+n]$ can differ by more than $\lvert A \rvert$. So it is not an easy problem in general (for $\lvert A \rvert$ small it indeed is)
"Also the cardinalities of $B\cap [1,n]$ and $B\cap [k+1,k+n]$ can differ by more than |A|." Is that for the example of $A$ that you gave, with $k=|A|$? Then they would differ by about $|A|/2$.

No, for another example (or $k,n$ are used multiple times). The translation can be with a huge difference. E.g. for a set $A=\{a,b,c\}$ of size $3$, one can translate such that the number of multiples of $a,b,c$ and $abc$ is one larger, while the number of multiples of $ab,ac,bc$ is the same (here $a,b,c$ are pairwise relative prime). So that example was just to indicate that the problem is far from trivial.

I agree that this was simply misstated and should be corrected. Some more evidence for this is that the question is also discussed in problem E5 of Guy's collection [Gu04]. And there it is correctly stated. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I agree with everyone that this problem statement was just a typo, and it meant to ask for multiples rather than non-multiples. I will just correct the statement and note the non-multiple problem in the remarks; I don't think it warrants creating a separate problem, since it was likely never actually a question intended by Erdős.

I agree that Erdős never intended the incorrect statement, and so one can imagine that as a collection of "ideal" Erdős problems (the volume on the shelf next to "The Book", which has all the solutions?), the incorrect statement doesn't belong here. Nonetheless, I would like to (softly) present two alternate arguments. First, we already have comments here and (importantly) material external to the site that address the problem as written. For example, I posted a Lean proof on GitHub and of course there's the Formal Conjectures project as well as the community database. (I also have existing personal communication and documents that reference the current problem.) Second, Erdős did in fact write this problem down incorrectly in a paper. This is not just a misformalization by someone else, but by Erdős himself -- in an immutable medium. Perhaps both arguments are philosophically about what it means to be an "archival" resource. (These arguments also apply to #124, but there's a difference of degree. This problem was already labeled as solved a long time ago, and there's an accepted formal proof, etc. I accept if your view differs and feel free to do what you think is right with both problems.)

Both reasonable points. I never intended this site to be too strict an archival resource; rather something that begins with many inevitable errors and typos that slowly converges to be an accurate compilation of Erdős problems. I do now avoid e.g. renumbering of problems, conscious that these have become a somewhat standardised way of referring to some problems, used by a number of other sites and papers. In instances where there is an error in the problem statement that is then repeated in e.g. a formalised statement, but then pointed out here, I still think that the error should be corrected here (and then hopefully this correction can propagate through the ecosystem eventually), and a comment in this section can serve as an archival record of the changes made. This is an interesting case though, since as you say technically Erdős did ask the question as written here - it's just he almost surely did not intend to! And I do want to be careful about speculating too much about what he was thinking, lest this site become more "my impression of Erdős" (inevitably very partial and flawed) rather than what Erdős himself said. However, there are a number of precedents of multiple very similar questions forming a single problem on this site, which I think this falls under. So I think it might be most appropriate to list both versions in this problem, noting the likely typo - then external references to this problem still make sense. Thoughts from others welcome!

For what it's worth, I agree with your proposed solution of correcting the problem statement (and changing it from disproved to open), and noting the other (presumed misstated) version as a comment below. By some coincidence I just found this 1966 paper in Hungarian, where Erdős (see page 150, or 16 of the pdf-file) also mentioned this problem! And lo and behold, here he does state the correct version. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

[Note: this comment was written before 2025/12/01, when the problem text was updated.] The smallest counterexample appears to be $A=\{2, 3, 5, 7\}$, $n=9$, and $m=13$, which has $\lvert B\cap[1,n]\rvert=\lvert\{1\}\rvert=1$ and $\lvert B\cap[1,m]\rvert=\lvert\{1,11,13\}\rvert=3$, but $\frac{3}{13} > 2\cdot\frac{1}{9}$.

[Note: this comment was written before 2025/12/01, when the problem text was updated.] This problem has a formalized statement available at the Formal Conjectures project. Aristotle from Harmonic found its own counterexample for this problem, provided only with that Lean statement! My understanding is that this was entirely by itself, without the information on this page. Type-check it online! Its counterexample is $n=13$, $m=200$, and $A=\{2,3,5,7,11,13\}$. Some comments: 1. I still think Erdős mis-stated the conjecture accidentally, and the correct statement should be added as another problem. 2. Aristotle has shown that it can find mis-statements and counterexamples. (See also here.) It appears to be a useful tool to make sure you have stated your conjecture correctly, especially including necessary hypotheses and negations! 3. Of course, this would be more useful if it found a counterexample to a problem where a human hasn't already. Hopefully soon! (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
